result.markdown:
--- title: "Proposed Solutions — Closing the Unrecognized Gap" format: html: toc: true toc-depth: 3 --- ## Overview of the Unrecognized Population The 53\,754 rows that Step 5 could not resolve fall into three buckets, each requiring a different strategy: | Root cause | Rows | Strategy | |------------|-----:|----------| | No / zero purchase history | 47 | External enrichment (agentic search **or** catalogue match) | | Very low purchase history (1–4 units) | 31\,400 | Similar-product transfer + analyst review | | Some history but price out of range | 21\,900 | Threshold relaxation / cluster expansion | : {.striped .hover} --- ## Solution A — Agentic Web Search for Zero-History Products ### The problem When a product has **no purchase history at all**, Step 5 has no anchors to compute proximity against — no DBSCAN cluster, no WAP, nothing. The only signal available is the product description itself. ### Proposed approach An agentic pipeline can augment these rows autonomously: 1. **Query construction** — from `RecognizedProductDesc`, `RecognizedManufacturerName`, and `RecognizedProductCatalogNumber` build a structured search query (e.g., `"Abbott 1192ANS Anchor SwiftLock unit of measure"` or a medical-device pricing database lookup). 2. **Search & parse** — the agent calls a web search tool or a medical product catalogue API and extracts candidate UOM labels and typical pack sizes. 3. **Citation attachment** — every suggestion is stored with its source URL and snippet so an analyst can verify it before accepting. 4. **Confidence flagging** — the agent rates its own confidence (e.g., "found exact catalog number" vs. "matched by description keyword"). Low-confidence suggestions are flagged for mandatory human review before write-back. ### What needs to be built and tested - Prompt engineering to reliably extract structured UOM data from unstructured web pages. - A de-duplication pass to collapse near-identical suggestions. - A review UI that surfaces the citation alongside the suggested UOM for analyst sign-off. - A feedback loop: accepted suggestions are written back into the purchase history so future runs can resolve the same products without another search. ### Trade-offs | Benefit | Risk | |---------|------| | Extends coverage to truly novel products | Hallucination / incorrect web data — must require analyst approval | | Provides auditable citations | Adds latency and API cost per zero-history product | | Self-improving over time as accepted results grow history | Needs prompt fine-tuning per product category | --- ## Solution B — Similar-Product Transfer (Purchase History Bridge) ### The problem The 31\,400 rows with very low purchase history have *some* data but not enough to build a reliable DBSCAN cluster or meaningful WAP. ### Proposed approach Rather than going to the web, look inside the existing purchase history for **similar products**: 1. **Similarity candidates** — for a product with sparse history, find other products in the catalogue that share: - Same `RecognizedManufacturerID` - Same `RecognizedProductTypeID` - Similar description tokens (keyword overlap or embedding distance) 2. **UOM profile transfer** — the similar product's ThresholdV2 clusters and WAP are borrowed as a *prior*. The proximity scoring in Step 5 runs exactly as normal, just with the borrowed thresholds substituting for the absent ones. 3. **Downgraded confidence** — because the thresholds are borrowed, the resulting confidence score is multiplied by a transfer discount factor (e.g., 0.80) to signal to downstream consumers that this is an inferred — not directly observed — assignment. 4. **Analyst review trigger** — rows resolved via transfer are surfaced in a separate review queue so analysts can confirm the analogy is valid for that product category. ### What needs to be discussed with analysts - **Acceptable similarity criteria** — does same manufacturer + same product type constitute a strong enough analogy? Are there categories where this would be systematically misleading (e.g., two products with the same type ID but very different pricing tiers)? - **Discount factor calibration** — how much should transferred confidence be penalised? - **Segment sensitivity** — certain clinical categories (e.g., implants vs. consumables) may require different similarity rules. ### Trade-offs | Benefit | Risk | |---------|------| | Uses existing, trusted purchase data — no external calls | Wrong analogy can propagate a systematic error | | Fast — no added latency | Similarity is hard to define without analyst input | | Gracefully degrades to unrecognized if no similar product found | Requires a new similarity index to be maintained | --- ## Solution C — Threshold Relaxation for Out-of-Range Prices ### The problem The 21\,900 rows with adequate history fail because their file `PriceUOM` falls outside *every* DBSCAN cluster at a distance where the proximity score is below 0.30. Common causes: - **Supplier price change** — the vendor raised or lowered prices since the last purchase, shifting the quote outside the historical band. - **New pack size** — the supplier is now selling in a quantity not previously ordered (e.g., BX/24 when history only has BX/12 and BX/100). - **Currency / unit mismatch** — a price-per-case was accidentally quoted as price-per-each. ### Proposed approaches #### C1 — Widened acceptance window (lower LOG_EPS threshold) `LOG_EPS = 0.25` currently corresponds to a roughly 1.8× tolerance from the cluster edge. Increasing it to `0.40` extends tolerance to ~2.5×. This is a global change and must be evaluated on a hold-out set to measure false-positive impact. #### C2 — Price-trend adjustment For products with a time-stamped purchase history, the system can detect upward or downward price trends and project the cluster range forward. A product whose EA price has trended from $10 → $13 → $16 over 12 months is likely priced around $19 today; the cluster built on 12-month-old data will be offset. #### C3 — Analyst escalation queue Rows where the best candidate score is 0.20–0.30 (just below acceptance) are surfaced in a dedicated review queue. The analyst sees the best DBSCAN candidate, its score, and the distance from the cluster edge — making a quick accept/reject decision fast. Accepted overrides are recorded and used to retrain the threshold or expand the cluster. --- ## Prioritisation Matrix ::: {#ebd69244 .cell execution_count=2} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: | Solution | Potential reach | Implementation effort | Requires analyst input | |----------|----------------:|-----------------------:|------------------------| | A — Agentic search | 47 rows (zero-history) | High — prompt engineering + review UI | Yes — citation sign-off | | B — Similar-product transfer | 31\,400 rows (low history) | Medium — similarity index + discount logic | Yes — similarity criteria definition | | C1 — Widen LOG_EPS | Subset of 21\,900 rows | Low — one config change + validation | No — but needs hold-out evaluation | | C3 — Escalation queue | Near-miss rows (score 0.20–0.30) | Low — UI change only | Yes — per-row review | : {.striped .hover} --- ## Recommended Starting Point 1. **Immediate** — implement **C3 (escalation queue)** to capture near-misses with minimal engineering. This recovers the easiest unrecognized rows and builds analyst feedback data that can be used to calibrate all other solutions. 2. **Short term** — evaluate **C1 (LOG_EPS relaxation)** on a hold-out set. If the false-positive rate is acceptable, deploy it to recover a large portion of the out-of-range rows automatically. 3. **Medium term** — design and pilot **Solution B (similar-product transfer)** with analysts. The similarity criteria need to be co-designed with domain experts before any automated write-back. 4. **Longer term** — prototype **Solution A (agentic search)** on a small batch of zero-history products, evaluate citation quality, and build the review workflow before scaling.
